title: Nail Polish on Silk
stainChemistry: Nail polish is a nitrocellulose film colored with insoluble pigments suspended in ester solvents (ethyl acetate, butyl acetate) and small amounts of acetone. On silk, the polish film bonds mechanically to the protein fiber surface while the solvent evaporates. Silk is a delicate protein fiber (fibroin + sericin) whose structure is damaged by water spots, friction, and most household solvents. Acetone does not dissolve silk but causes silk to lose its soft hand and can create permanent rings and color bleed. Isopropyl alcohol strips silk dyes. The very same solvents that would reverse the polish film damage the silk substrate.
whyThisWorks: Amyl acetate applied by a professional spotter is the only fiber-safe route for polish on silk. Amyl acetate softens nitrocellulose without affecting protein fibers, and a trained spotter blots in tight concentric passes, follows with NSD to lift pigment, then neutralizes with dilute acetic acid under pH control. This requires professional chemistry, fiber-testing, and moisture control silk cannot tolerate at home. Consumer-grade 'non-acetone' nail polish removers contain unregulated ester concentrations and are not safe on silk even with testing.
defaultAssumption: Assume the customer does NOT have amyl acetate or professional spotting experience. Default recommendation is professional dry-clean referral. Do not offer consumer-tier DIY chemical suggestions on this card.

spottingProtocol.instruction: This combination is a professional-only case. Take the garment to a dry cleaner with silk-spotting experience and tell them: (1) the stain is nail polish, (2) the fiber is silk, (3) you have NOT applied any solvent, remover, or water at home. Do not use acetone, do not use 'non-acetone' polish remover, do not use rubbing alcohol, and do not attempt to rinse the stain with water — all of these cause permanent damage to silk. A professional will use amyl acetate and NSD under controlled conditions.

steps: 1. Fiber test: Apply amyl acetate to a hidden seam for 30 seconds. Check for sheen loss, ring formation, or color bleed. If any occurs, stop — refer to silk damage protocol.

2. Apply amyl acetate to the polish film with a clean white cloth, tight blotting motion in concentric passes from stain edge inward. 5-10 min total dwell, refreshing the cloth each pass. Do NOT flood silk.
3. Apply NSD (neutral synthetic detergent) to remove remaining pigment residue. Sponge gently with minimum moisture, blot dry between passes. 3-5 min.
4. Apply acetic acid 28% diluted 1:10 via dropper or micro-application for pH restoration. 1-2 min. Blot.
5. Air dry flat on absorbent padding. Do NOT use heat, do NOT hang wet. Re-inspect for any residual sheen loss or ring before returning to customer.
warnings: NEVER use acetone on silk — causes permanent hand loss and ring formation
NEVER use nail polish remover (acetone OR non-acetone) on silk — uncontrolled ester concentrations damage fibroin
NEVER use isopropyl alcohol — strips silk dyes
NEVER flood silk with water — permanent ring formation
Always fiber-test amyl acetate on a hidden seam

neverDo.reason: Causes permanent sheen loss, hand change, and ring formation on silk. Even brief contact is visible.
Both formulations contain ester solvents in uncontrolled concentrations. Damage silk fibroin.
Strips silk dyes. Permanent color loss.
Creates permanent rings on silk. Water spots are as visible as the polish stain.

safetyMatrix.customerAdvice: Do not attempt home removal. Do not apply ANY liquid — water will ring the silk. Take to a professional dry cleaner with silk experience. Tell them the fiber is silk and the stain is nail polish.

escalation.whenToEscalate: Immediately — this is a professional-only case.
escalation.whatToTell: Take to a dry cleaner experienced with silk. Tell them: nail polish on silk, no home treatment attempted, no water applied. Do not use acetone, do not use 'non-acetone' polish remover, do not use rubbing alcohol. Professional amyl acetate spotting under moisture control is the only safe route.

timeEstimate: Professional only — do not attempt at home
aftercare: If the polish is still wet, blot the excess gently from the outside of the stain inward with a dry white cloth. Do NOT rub, do NOT press the polish deeper into the weave, and do NOT apply any liquid — water will create permanent rings on silk. If the polish has already dried, skip to step 2.

homeSolutions.agent: On silk, the honest home answer is: don't
homeSolutions.instruction: Nail polish on silk is the rare stain where no home solvent is safe. Acetone strips the soft hand and creates permanent rings. "Non-acetone" removers contain ester solvents in uncontrolled concentrations that bleed silk dyes. Isopropyl alcohol strips silk color. Water alone does nothing to nitrocellulose polish and CAN leave permanent water rings on silk. Your best move is to leave the stain alone and get it to a silk-safe pro as fast as possible — ideally within 24 hours, while it's still softer.

homeSolutions.agent: If wet, blot gently — once, then stop
homeSolutions.instruction: If the polish is still liquid, press a dry white cloth straight down on the pool, outside edges toward the center. Lift straight up — no wiping, no smearing. Two or three light dabs to pick up surface liquid, then stop. Do NOT add water, do NOT rub, do NOT press deeper into the weave. You're just removing what's sitting on top.

homeSolutions.agent: If dry, leave it completely alone
homeSolutions.instruction: A hardened nail polish deposit on silk is EASIER for a pro to treat than one that's been worked on. Do NOT pick at it with a fingernail. Do NOT scrape with a knife or card — scraping tears silk fibroin. Do NOT attempt to soften it with heat, water, or anything else. Silk is fragile; leave the polish in place.

homeSolutions.agent: Go to a silk specialist — confirm by phone first
homeSolutions.instruction: Not every dry cleaner can handle silk. Call ahead and ask: "Do you treat silk with amyl acetate for nail polish?" If they don't understand the question, try another cleaner. When you bring it in, tell them: "Nail polish on silk — [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing." That exact handoff matters — pros use amyl acetate with strict fiber-testing and pH control, and knowing you haven't compromised the fiber lets them go straight to the safe treatment.
